nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich shallow coastal reefs, typically at depths of around 2 to 16 m. Adults are sometimes seen in small aggregations.

diet.notes: In the wild this species has been documented to feed almost exclusively on coral polyps, making it an obligate corallivore. Captive specimens are notoriously difficult to wean onto prepared foods and have a poor track record of long-term survival on aquarium diets. When acclimation is attempted, it is generally advised to offer a varied mix of live and frozen meaty preparations (mysis, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped clam or shrimp), Aiptasia, and herbivore preparations multiple times per day, ideally introduced into a mature system with abundant live rock for grazing.

avoidWith: live corals (will nip and consume polyps)
other butterflyfish unless a bonded pair

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 5653x3769. Tack-sharp in-situ reef shot showing whole fish, vivid yellow-orange body, jet-black dorsal/anal/caudal fins, and dark facial bar through eye - all diagnostic features clear.
Diego Delso; 5323x3634. Crisp lateral in-situ view of a different vibrant individual; strong contrast between yellow body and black fins.
Diego Delso; 4358x2905. Sharp in-situ shot, deeper orange tone; full body and facial bars visible. Slightly cropped tail versus the recommended pick.

summary: The Arabian Butterflyfish (Chaetodon melapterus) is a small Western Indian Ocean species recognized by its yellow-orange body, dark facial bars, and jet-black fins. It is generally considered an expert-only species due to its specialized corallivorous diet and poor track record in captivity.
careNotes:
The Arabian Butterflyfish is endemic to the Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, southern Red Sea, and Gulf of Aden, where it tends to inhabit coral-rich shallow reefs at depths of around 2 to 16 m. In the wild it feeds almost exclusively on hard- and soft-coral polyps as an obligate corallivore, which makes it one of the more difficult butterflyfishes to maintain long-term in captivity. Captive survival prospects are generally considered very poor without an extremely well-established system and a skilled feeding regime.

Because of its obligate coral-polyp diet, this species is not reef-safe — it is known to consume LPS (large-polyp stony) and soft coral polyps, fan worms, and may pick at Tridacna clams. It is best housed in a mature fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) system with abundant live rock, plentiful grazing surfaces, and ample open swimming space. Some specimens will eventually accept enriched brine shrimp, mysis, finely chopped seafood, Aiptasia, and algae-based preparations, but many refuse prepared foods entirely, so a target-feeding routine and a dedicated acclimation period are commonly advised.

Like most butterflyfishes, this species tends to form monogamous pairs in the wild. In aquariums it is generally advised to keep a single specimen or an established mated pair, with both fish introduced simultaneously.
breedingNotes: The Arabian Butterflyfish has been reported to form monogamous pairs in the wild that spawn via pelagic (free-floating) eggs. Captive breeding has not been meaningfully achieved for this species, as is the case for most butterflyfishes — the pelagic larvae are very difficult to rear, and adults present significant challenges even for basic long-term maintenance in aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care/husbandry profile: 55 gal min tank, 5 in max size, peaceful temperament, expert-only care, not reef safe, omnivore including Aiptasia and meaty/algae foods, 75-82°F, pH 8.0-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used in place of the designated Reef2Reef thread (which returned HTTP 403). Provided 13 cm TL max size, wild temperature 25.2-29.3°C (mean 27.1°C), distribution (Western Indian Ocean: Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, southern Red Sea of Yemen), reef-associated/non-migratory habitat, taxonomy (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes), and importantly the obligate corallivore feeding ecology.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Specialist hobby encyclopedia. Cross-checked size (13 cm), temperature (25-28°C), depth (2-16 m), expanded country list, and recommended minimum tank of 1500 L (used as the upper end of the source spread for the tank-size decision). Reinforced the FishBase view that this is a near-obligate coral-polyp feeder with very poor captive survival.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Wholesale care profile. Cross-checked min tank size (75 gal / 284 L), 5 in adult size, temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, and the consensus that the species is not reef safe and may pick at corals and crustaceans.

fish.breedingNotes: Forms monogamous pairs in the wild and is oviparous, releasing pelagic larvae that drift in open water. Captive rearing of the pelagic-larval stage has not been meaningfully achieved for this species.
